Steam Recommender System

Recommending Games Based on User Playtime

Data Science
Machine Learning
Recommender Systems
Video Games
Recommending Games Based on User Playtime
Author

Matthew Burns

Published

January 19, 2026

TL;DR

This project builds a collaborative-filtering recommender system for Steam games using an implicit-feedback Alternating Least Squares (ALS) model. The workflow is documented as a short series so readers can scan the why, what I did, and what came out without digging through the entire repo.

Quick links

  • GitHub repo
  • Notebooks (read in order):
    1. Part 1 — Data Collection
    2. Part 2 — Data Preparation
    3. Part 3 — Implicit ALS Modeling

What this demonstrates

  • End-to-end DS workflow: data ingestion → cleaning/reshaping → modeling → evaluation
  • Working with real-world “messy” nested data (Steam user-item histories)
  • Recommender-system fundamentals:
    • Implicit feedback (playtime) vs explicit ratings
    • Sparse user-item matrices
    • ALS factorization and practical tuning
  • Pragmatic model validation using ranking metrics (NDCG@K)

Project overview

Goal

Recommend Steam games to users using interaction history (playtime). Because playtime is not a 1-5 rating, this is treated as implicit feedback where “more playtime” indicates stronger preference.

Data source

This project uses a public Steam interactions dataset released by UCSD’s recommender-systems group (McAuley Lab). The raw data comes as a gzipped JSON-lines file containing users and their nested item histories.

Approach in one paragraph

I download and parse the raw dataset, convert the nested structures into a clean long-form table (user, item, playtime), apply practical filtering and transformations (cold-start mitigation + log playtime), generate consistent numeric IDs, create train/validation/test splits, then train and tune an implicit ALS model and evaluate ranking quality with NDCG@10.

Walkthrough (3 parts)

Part 1 — Data Collection

Focus: reproducible ingestion

  • Download the dataset
  • Extract from GZIP
  • Parse JSON-by-line structure
  • Save to a local artifact for reuse

Open Part 1

Part 2 — Data Preparation

Focus: “model-ready” interactions table

  • Expand nested user → items structure into long form
  • Fix/standardize user identifiers
  • Drop very-low-interaction users (reduce cold-start impact)
  • Transform playtime (log(1 + x))
  • Map user/item IDs to contiguous integers
  • Build samples + train/valid/test splits

Open Part 2

Part 3 — Implicit ALS Modeling

Focus: collaborative filtering

  • Build a sparse user-item matrix
  • Train ALS (implicit feedback formulation)
  • Grid-search key hyperparameters:
    • latent factors
    • regularization
    • alpha (confidence scaling)
  • Evaluate ranking quality (NDCG@10)
  • Train a final model configuration

Open Part 3

Key implementation details

Cold-start mitigation

To avoid training a model dominated by extremely sparse users, I filter out users with too few interactions before training. This improves recommendation stability and makes evaluation more meaningful.

Implicit feedback handling

Playtime is treated as a confidence-weighted preference signal rather than an explicit rating. I also apply a log transform to dampen heavy-tail effects (a few games with extremely high playtime).

Evaluation

I measure ranking quality using NDCG@10 on a held-out validation set during tuning, then use the test set for final sanity checks.

How to run (locally)

  1. Clone the repo
  2. Create the environment (requirements/conda)
  3. Run notebooks in order:
    • 01-Data-Collection.ipynb
    • 02-Data-Preparation.ipynb
    • 03-Implicit-ALS.ipynb
Source Code

## TL;DR

This project builds a **collaborative-filtering recommender system** for Steam
games using an **implicit-feedback Alternating Least Squares (ALS)** model. The
workflow is documented as a short series so readers can scan the *why*,
*what I did*, and *what came out* without digging through the entire repo.

## Quick links

- [GitHub repo](https://github.com/msburns24/Steam-Recommender-System)
- **Notebooks (read in order):**
  1. [Part 1 — Data Collection](Part-1-Data-Collection.ipynb)
  2. [Part 2 — Data Preparation](Part-2-Data-Preparation.ipynb)
  3. [Part 3 — Implicit ALS Modeling](Part-3-Implicit-ALS.ipynb)

## What this demonstrates

- End-to-end DS workflow: **data ingestion → cleaning/reshaping → modeling → evaluation**
- Working with real-world "messy" nested data (Steam user-item histories)
- Recommender-system fundamentals:
  - **Implicit feedback** (playtime) vs explicit ratings
  - Sparse user-item matrices
  - ALS factorization and practical tuning
- Pragmatic model validation using ranking metrics (**NDCG@K**)

## Project overview

### Goal

Recommend Steam games to users using interaction history (playtime). Because
playtime is not a 1-5 rating, this is treated as **implicit feedback** where
"more playtime" indicates stronger preference.

### Data source

This project uses a public Steam interactions dataset released by UCSD's
recommender-systems group (McAuley Lab). The raw data comes as a gzipped
JSON-lines file containing users and their nested item histories.

### Approach in one paragraph

I download and parse the raw dataset, convert the nested structures into a
clean long-form table (user, item, playtime), apply practical filtering and
transformations (cold-start mitigation + log playtime), generate consistent
numeric IDs, create train/validation/test splits, then train and tune an
**implicit ALS** model and evaluate ranking quality with **NDCG@10**.

## Walkthrough (3 parts)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### Part 1 — Data Collection

**Focus:** reproducible ingestion

- Download the dataset
- Extract from GZIP
- Parse JSON-by-line structure
- Save to a local artifact for reuse

[Open Part 1](Part-1-Data-Collection.ipynb)
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Part 2 — Data Preparation

**Focus:** "model-ready" interactions table

- Expand nested user → items structure into long form
- Fix/standardize user identifiers
- Drop very-low-interaction users (reduce cold-start impact)
- Transform playtime (log(1 + x))
- Map user/item IDs to contiguous integers
- Build samples + train/valid/test splits

[Open Part 2](Part-2-Data-Preparation.ipynb)
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Part 3 — Implicit ALS Modeling

**Focus:** collaborative filtering

- Build a sparse user-item matrix
- Train ALS (implicit feedback formulation)
- Grid-search key hyperparameters:
  - latent factors
  - regularization
  - alpha (confidence scaling)
- Evaluate ranking quality (NDCG@10)
- Train a final model configuration

[Open Part 3](Part-3-Implicit-ALS.ipynb)
:::
:::

## Key implementation details

### Cold-start mitigation

To avoid training a model dominated by extremely sparse users, I filter out
users with too few interactions before training. This improves recommendation
stability and makes evaluation more meaningful.

### Implicit feedback handling

Playtime is treated as a confidence-weighted preference signal rather than an
explicit rating. I also apply a log transform to dampen heavy-tail effects (a
few games with extremely high playtime).

### Evaluation

I measure ranking quality using **NDCG@10** on a held-out validation set during
tuning, then use the test set for final sanity checks.

## How to run (locally)

1. Clone the repo
2. Create the environment (requirements/conda)
3. Run notebooks in order:
   - `01-Data-Collection.ipynb`
   - `02-Data-Preparation.ipynb`
   - `03-Implicit-ALS.ipynb`
